title: Excess sweating and body odour
excerpt: Excess sweating and body odour trouble most of us, especially through the hot months. Ayurveda names this condition Atisvedana and traces it, with practical precision, to Pitta dosha and the body's own cooling channels.
at_a_glance: Excess sweating (Atisvedana) is a Pitta imbalance in the body's sweat channels, worsened by spicy food, heat, and stress.
Body odour intensifies when Ama (digestive residue) enters the sweat channels, making diet the first place to intervene.
Cooling herbs like sandalwood and vetiver, breathable fabrics, and a Pitta-calming diet are the classical first steps.

body.heading: when your body turns up the heat
paragraphs: Most of us know the feeling: a crowded metro, a spicy lunch, an anxious moment before a presentation, and suddenly the shirt is damp, the palms are wet, and there is a smell you wish others could not catch. Sweating is, of course, entirely natural. Ayurveda does not treat it as a problem in itself. What it watches is excess: too much sweat (Atisvedana), sweat that smells sharply (Durgandha Sveda), and the discomfort that follows both.

The classical view is that sweat, called Sveda in Sanskrit, is a byproduct of fat tissue (Medas). As the body digests and metabolises fat, moisture is released through the skin's channels, the Svedavaha Srotas. These channels are governed largely by Pitta dosha, the principle of heat and transformation, and by Vata, which drives movement. When Pitta is aggravated, whether by hot weather, pungent food, emotional intensity, or irregular sleep, the channels work overtime and sweat becomes excessive (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana).
body.pullQuote: when Ama enters the sweat channels, the resulting odour is noticeably more pungent

body.heading: what the granthas say about smell
paragraphs: Charaka describes Sveda among the waste products (Mala) of the body and notes that when it accumulates or is not cleared properly, it contributes to skin conditions and unpleasant odour (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). The odour itself, Durgandha, arises when Pitta's sharp, slightly sour quality mixes with the metabolic waste sitting in the channels. Ama, the sticky residue of poor digestion, can make this worse: when Ama enters the sweat channels, the resulting odour is noticeably more pungent (Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana).

Bhavaprakasha, the later compendium that many household Ayurvedic remedies draw from, lists several herbs that act on Svedavaha Srotas and on Pitta: Chandana (sandalwood), Ushira (vetiver root), and Lodhra among them. These are considered Pittashamaka, meaning they reduce Pitta's excess heat, and also mild Stambhana agents that gently reduce secretion without blocking it entirely (Bhavaprakasha Nighantu). The distinction matters: Ayurveda does not want to stop sweating, only to bring it back to its natural, balanced level.

Sushruta adds a useful anatomical note: there are as many sweat pores as there are hair follicles, making skin a continuous surface of temperature regulation. Disorders of the Svedavaha Srotas include both Atisvedana (excess) and Asvedana (absence of sweat), and both are considered imbalances to correct rather than ignore (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana).

body.heading: everyday habits that help
paragraphs: Diet is the first place to look. Pitta is aggravated by sour, salty, pungent, and fermented foods. If you sweat heavily or notice a sharp body odour, reducing very spicy curries, pickles, alcohol, and coffee, especially in summer, gives the channels room to settle. Favour cooling foods: cucumber, coriander-leaf chutney, coconut water, fennel seeds after meals, and plain buttermilk (takra) over curd at night.

A daily bath with a handful of Ushira (vetiver) steeped in warm water, or a few drops of Chandana (sandalwood) added to the rinse, can visibly reduce odour within a week. Lodhra or Triphala powder rubbed gently on sweating areas and rinsed off is another traditional approach that tightens the pores and balances the local Pitta. These are kitchen-and-pharmacy remedies with long use behind them.

Breathable, natural fabrics, cotton in summer and linen when possible, let the skin do its regulatory work without trapping the Sveda against the body. Synthetic fabrics hold heat and moisture and amplify odour. This is not just common sense: Ayurveda speaks of the importance of keeping the skin clean and the pores open so waste exits naturally rather than accumulating (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

Sleep before midnight, minimal screen time after dark, and a short Sheetali pranayama (the cooling breath, done by curling the tongue and inhaling slowly) each morning are among the simplest Pitta-pacifying practices one can sustain year-round. They work slowly but reliably. The goal in Ayurveda is not to chase symptoms but to adjust the underlying heat so the symptoms stop arising.

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: This article is general information and not a substitute for medical care or a qualified vaidya's guidance. See a doctor if sweating is sudden and unexplained, if it wakes you from sleep regularly (night sweats can signal infection or hormonal changes), if it is confined to one side of the body, if it is accompanied by chest pain, shortness of breath, or fever, or if it comes with significant unintended weight loss. Body odour that changes suddenly and is very different from your normal smell, or any skin redness, rash, or lesion in the affected area, also warrants a clinical check. A qualified Ayurvedic vaidya should be consulted for personalised advice on diet, herbs, and Panchakarma, particularly if symptoms persist despite simple dietary changes.

try_this_today.title: Vetiver bath rinse
try_this_today.description: Steep a small handful of dried Ushira (vetiver root) in warm water for 10 minutes. Use this as your final rinse after bathing. Do it daily in summer for a week and notice the change in odour.

try_this_today.title: Fennel seeds after meals
try_this_today.description: Chew a quarter teaspoon of roasted fennel seeds after lunch and dinner. Fennel is cooling, aids digestion, and reduces the Ama build-up that worsens sweat odour.

try_this_today.title: Sheetali pranayama in the morning
try_this_today.description: Curl your tongue into a tube (or keep lips slightly parted if you cannot curl), inhale slowly through the mouth for a count of 5, close the mouth, hold briefly, and exhale through the nose. Ten rounds each morning cool Pitta over time.

try_this_today.title: Reduce the Pitta triggers first
try_this_today.description: For two weeks, cut spicy pickles, excess coffee, alcohol, and very sour foods. Keep a simple note of how your sweating changes. This is a quick way to confirm whether Pitta is the driving factor for you.

try_this_today.title: Coconut water mid-morning
try_this_today.description: A glass of tender coconut water between 10 am and noon, when Pitta is naturally at its peak, is one of the simplest and most effective cooling habits from classical practice.

ingredients.name: Fennel
ingredients.description: Cooling and digestive; reduces Ama that worsens sweat odour when chewed after meals

ingredients.name: Coriander
ingredients.description: A classic Pitta-pacifying herb; fresh coriander chutney or coriander-seed water cools the system

words_to_know.definition: Sweat; in Ayurveda it is classified as a natural waste product (Mala) of fat tissue
Excess sweating; a disorder of the Svedavaha Srotas (sweat channels)
The channels that carry and regulate sweat through the skin
Sticky, undigested metabolic residue that can enter channels and worsen odour and inflammation
Herbs or practices that reduce excess Pitta (heat) in the body
Sweat with an unpleasant odour; a recognised condition in classical Ayurvedic texts

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana · Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana · Bhavaprakasha, Purvakhanda (Nighantu)
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
